quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Emission spectrum"?

options.text: Bright colored lines on a dark background; produced by excited atoms emitting photons at characteristic frequencies
v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself
The image from lens 1 serves as the object for lens 2; d_o2 = separation - d_i1; apply thin lens equation sequentially
F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Emission spectrum" is defined as: Bright colored lines on a dark background; produced by excited atoms emitting photons at characteristic frequencies. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Absorption spectrum"?

options.text: Dark lines on a continuous bright background; produced when cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths from white light
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂; same current through each. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂; same voltage across each
Zero electrical resistance below a critical temperature T_c; enables powerful electromagnets (MRI: 1.5-7T fields) and lossless power transmission
When a driving frequency matches a natural frequency of the tube, standing waves form and the sound is amplified dramatically

quiz_questions.explanation: "Absorption spectrum" is defined as: Dark lines on a continuous bright background; produced when cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths from white light. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Spectral fingerprint"?

options.text: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ...; total voltage = sum of individual voltages; same charge on each capacitor
High-voltage transmission minimizes I²R losses: P_loss = I²R = P²R/V²; higher V → lower I → much lower losses
Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)
Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars

quiz_questions.explanation: "Spectral fingerprint" is defined as: Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rydberg formula"?

options.text: v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself
Uses r = mv/(qB) to separate ions by mass: heavier ions curve less (larger r), lighter ions curve more (smaller r)
1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rydberg formula" is defined as: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Continuous spectrum"?

options.text: ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops
E = hf = hc/λ; h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant); higher frequency = higher energy per photon
A quantum system exists in multiple states simultaneously until measured; the wave function collapses to one state upon observation
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Continuous spectrum" is defined as: Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Bright colored lines on a dark background; produced by excited atoms emitting photons at characteristic frequencies"?

options.text: Emission spectrum
Dielectric constant (κ)
Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Malus's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Emission spectrum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Dark lines on a continuous bright background; produced when cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths from white light"?

options.text: Combined power of thin lenses in contact
Absorption spectrum
Total internal reflection
Rayleigh criterion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Absorption spectrum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars"?

options.text: Wien's displacement law
Spectral fingerprint
Standing waves
Curie temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Spectral fingerprint".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹"?

options.text: Multi-lens systems
Rydberg formula
Quantum mechanical model
Nuclear medicine

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rydberg formula".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)"?

options.text: Continuous spectrum
Magnetic domains
Quantum tunneling
Magnetic field of a wire

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Continuous spectrum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Emission and Absorption Spectra is accurate?

options.text: Radioactive decay follows N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), with half-life t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ.
Emission and Absorption Spectra are spectroscopic fingerprints of elements.
Light as a Wave examines the wave nature of light in detail.
Fluids and Pressure examines the behavior of liquids and gases under pressure.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Emission and Absorption Spectra as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Emission and Absorption Spectra?

options.text: An emission spectrum consists of bright lines at specific wavelengths produced when excited gas atoms emit photons during electron transitions downward.
The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
Voltage (electric potential difference) is ΔV = ΔU/q = -∫E·ds.
Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Emission and Absorption Spectra as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Emission and Absorption Spectra?

options.text: Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
The energy level diagram shows allowed states; arrows between levels represent possible transitions.
Beta-plus decay (β⁺): proton → neutron + positron + neutrino (Z→Z-1).
An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Emission and Absorption Spectra as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Emission and Absorption Spectra?

options.text: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave produced by vibrating objects.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
Each element has a unique spectrum.
Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Emission and Absorption Spectra as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Emission and Absorption Spectra?

options.text: The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
The Rydberg formula for hydrogen: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²), where R_H = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.
Fission: a heavy nucleus (e.g., ²³⁵U) splits into lighter nuclei + neutrons + energy.
Fluids and Pressure examines the behavior of liquids and gases under pressure.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Emission and Absorption Spectra as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Emission and Absorption Spectra?

options.text: Night vision devices use the photoelectric effect: photons from dim scenes eject electrons from a photocathode, which are then amplified to produce a visible image
Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)
Stellar composition: absorption lines in starlight reveal which elements are in the star's atmosphere; helium was first discovered in the Sun's spectrum before being found on Earth
Tire pressure monitoring: as tires heat up during driving, Gay-Lussac's law predicts pressure increases at constant volume—tire pressure sensors alert drivers to dangerous changes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Emission and Absorption Spectra.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Emission and Absorption Spectra?

options.text: Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths
Gas identification: each gas produces unique spectral lines—used to identify unknown gases in chemistry labs, environmental monitoring, and forensics
Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Emission and Absorption Spectra.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Emission and Absorption Spectra applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)
The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
Nuclear power plants: controlled fission of ²³⁵U in fuel rods heats water to produce steam that drives turbines, generating ~20% of US electricity without direct CO₂ emissions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Emission and Absorption Spectra.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Emission and Absorption Spectra?

options.text: Scuba diving: at depth, the increased pressure compresses air in the lungs (Boyle's law); divers must ascend slowly to avoid decompression sickness (the bends)
Stellar composition: absorption lines in starlight reveal which elements are in the star's atmosphere; helium was first discovered in the Sun's spectrum before being found on Earth
Defibrillators deliver a large potential difference (~1000-3000 V) across the heart to reset its electrical rhythm during cardiac arrest
CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Emission and Absorption Spectra.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Emission and Absorption Spectra in practice?

options.text: Xerox copiers use electrostatic attraction: a charged drum attracts toner particles to form an image, then heat fuses the toner to paper
3D movies use two differently polarized images projected simultaneously; polarized glasses filter one image to each eye, creating stereoscopic depth perception
Gas identification: each gas produces unique spectral lines—used to identify unknown gases in chemistry labs, environmental monitoring, and forensics
Musical instrument design: the length and shape of brass instruments determine which harmonics are produced; a bugle plays only natural harmonics of its tube length

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Emission and Absorption Spectra.
